speaker: interviewer
text: December 18, 1996, interview with Aron Bell. You were moving to Naliboki Pushcha.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Naliboki Pushcha, it was already endless woods, endless swamps. Finally, somewhere, somehow, it got to the, to the Germans that there is a lot of partisans in the Naliboki Pushcha. And they decided to eliminate ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to leave the camp and run deeper into the woods. We had to cross swamps and then find a little island again [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a drier spot. And this is how we survived the attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: They attacked us, I think, for about a week or so. I remember it. And it stands in my eyes like, like yesterday, how the-- their artillery, their, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess, small missiles or the bullets of the artillery, they were flying over our head. Or they will, they would fall ahead of us or behind us. But some way, somehow, we had no casualties by pure luck. And the Nazis were very mechanized, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very clean dressed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They would not want to get dirty, I guess, in the swamps. Or they were somewhat afraid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they would, would not get off the main road. And this is the way we survived until the Russian army came in 1940-- middle of 1944--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you move with a camp that size? Did everybody just get up and go? Were there smaller groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you had, you had horse and buggies. If it's in the winter, you have, you have horses and sleds. Some walked. And whatever you had to take on the sled, you put on the sled, or on the buggy if it's in the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it winter when you moved across the swamp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this attack happened to be in the summer, was not winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you move across the swamp?

speaker: interviewee
text: All you do is carry your, carry your rifle, your armament. That's it. You leave everything else. Nothing you can carry, just to be dressed and you armaments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone have to be left behind? The old, the sick, how did you move them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was, [SIGHS] unfortunately, one incident which one of my brothers killed one young fellow from our partisans. He got shot for that. They were divided by groups. Let's say, these five or 10 young men with rifles, they take an X amount of women and children or older men, and then by groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an assignment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, was assigned to every few young men. And then one of my brothers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] passed at the end. If everybody went out of the camp. And he saw a woman and two children crying. He asked her, why would you-- why are you here? Why aren't you with your group?

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said that this, this group leader did not want to take her. And she belonged to his group. And this was the only Jewish man that my brother shot in order to keep order. Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He got shot for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the spot?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the spot, in front of, in front of [? their truck, ?] 300, 400, at least 300, 400 young men with rifles. They fight in front of the fighting group. This was the only one during the entire course of the war that my brothers did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was discipline enforced generally without courts and judges and--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the discipline. This was something he did, he did beyond any criticism, beyond any judgement, beyond-- it's an animal. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was discipline enforced for smaller offenses?

speaker: interviewee
text: If he has to stay-- what do you call-- on watch, he has to stay two hours, he will stay six hours. Or he was told that this is not, this is not a, a, a normal time. And don't do this anymore. This is unacceptable and this. And you'll be surprised how the people were civilized. There were never any rapes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There weren't any-- there were no rapes in our area. In other words, in the Bielski brigade, there were no rapes. If a woman wanted to be with him, to sleep with him, that's her business. But if she turned him down, he wouldn't force her. That was unacceptable. And don't forget, this is wartime. I mean, those are people with minds. They know it's wartime. If, if the rule is don't go force anybody to do anything, I mean, there was rules and regulations. You better live by that. Because a person's life means nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within 1,200 people, were there some that were undisciplined, couldn't obey?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, yes, the very tough ones, yes. They did jokes. They [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] would get drunk sometimes, you know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they disciplined? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, it was needed very little, discipline. All you had to be is told. Whoever is-- by your commander, whoever is in charge. If it's Pesach's group or if it's anybody else's group, he will tell him, look, this is unacceptable. Don't do this no more. Because if it will get to my brothers, you'll be in trouble. And there is, there was very little punishment there. Punishment is a bullet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said some people were getting drunk. Was there a lot of drinking going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so, a lot of drinking, yes. Without the alcohol, I don't think they would be able to-- see, as depends how much alcohol a person takes in and how he can handle, how much alcohol he can handle. Alcohol at times is very good, [BACKGROUND NOISE] very helpful. A lot of drinking was, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any major disagreements of some groups with the leadership?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was disagreements, as I told you before. They went and wrote in a letter to the high Russian command that my brothers did wrong things, were doing wrong things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the intention of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you get in a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] six Jews together, this is my personal opinion I can give you, if you'll get in six Jews together, they will have seven parties.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, personally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I lived OK, because-- with some of the families. I lived a lot with the Frydberg family, [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] used to eat a lot there, or other families. I had no problem to-- I was sort of privileged somewhat. So I had pretty good compared to others.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did you have pretty, pretty good compared to others?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Look, I grew up very quickly. I don't think I had a normal childhood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe a year into the war, and I was probably a man like anybody else. I had, I had no normal childhood. And I guess my best examples were my brothers. Some of them are good, some of them were bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I think most of them, I guess, at the end, they were good examples. But they were good for the country over there, for that part of the world, not for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I wouldn't want to go into that. It's too personal. I wouldn't want to go into that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How [BACKGROUND NOISE] do you perceive it now differently than you perceived it then, the state of being in the woods? Did you think about it at the time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as something that was going to end? Could you foresee a future?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. First of all, in a way, I was a very sad kid. I was a very sad kid from the, from the time [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that I have seen they beat my father up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since then, my sadness remained up until this minute, in a way. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] If I'll go into a restaurant, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't show this. It's not their fault. And if I won't act myself civilized, I'd be thrown out. So I tried to be civilized like everybody else is. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] But to give you the best example really, is [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I understand life. I accept as it comes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I lived on borrowed [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] time. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But I try to be as civilized as possible as other people around me are.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by civilized?

speaker: interviewee
text: To share if you have some good, to help others, take care of my immediate family. If it is possible to live nice together, try the best you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: We jumped ahead a little bit. How long in total did you stay in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: From October, '41, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till, I think, June or July-- it was warm-- 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian army liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I can tell you what I remember through my-- as I felt. I felt very sad, very miserable. I guess I was the most miserable kid in those days, as far as I'm concerned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] During the war, you try. You don't think about it because you trying to survive. Now the war is finished, you can go anyplace you want. You can go home. But there is no home. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There is no home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They killed my parents. And, and I remember the way my father was beaten up. I think the most tragedy in my life was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that remained with me throughout my life, how they beat up my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This I never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This I never forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know exactly how your parents perished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. They butchered about 5,000 people through the weekend. They start--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nowogrodek, Novogrudok.

speaker: interviewer
text: By what means did they do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] They digged two long holes like this. In between those two holes, there was a little [? path ?] that only one person could stand and go through that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The holes were-- that they digged-- were about 100 meters, the length. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they were three, four meters, the width. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they simply brought those people on this path between the two holes. And that would-- they machine gunned them when you got on this path between those two digged, deep holes. They machine gunned them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some fell in wounded, some dead. This is the way they were butchered, over 5,000 Jews in one weekend. And that's where I lost a few of my brothers and my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that outside of town? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a suburb outside, in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about that before you hid, exactly how they died? Or did you find out later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Found out later, within a couple of weeks after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went into the woods? Did you find out after you got into the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We were in the woods at that time already. If they arrested my parents, I had no one to be with at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they from the time that they arrested them until they took them out to shoot them? Where did they have them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, matter of a day or hours, maybe. In the ghetto, in Novogrudok, they got them all together, the Jews from Novogrudok and the-- from the whole area, from the-- all the farmers who all lived in villages, they got them all together. And that's when they butchered them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many of the Jews from the ghetto in Novogrudok escape to your brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, most of them. 80% of the-- from our brigade were from ghetto of Novogrudok. You see, they had already-- they had, they had-- I don't remember. I think they had three sort of organized, in Jewish they would call it [NON-ENGLISH]. In other words, that they were butchered so many thousands in one shot. Three times, they have done it. The first time was in '41. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then was one in '42. And I think the last one was in '43. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Most of the Jews that were in our brigade were from Novogrudok, 80%.

speaker: interviewer
text: After liberation, did you immediately leave the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Russian soldiers arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Of course, I saw all the Nazis running. And they were-- Russians-- the Russian soldiers arrived. Of course, it was a very-- I don't know even how to describe it. As a said, I can only tell you me, personally. I was a very sad individual, very sad kid. Because I knew you can go home. And there is no home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this thought never left me, that there was no home. That's it, forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And what it's going to be tomorrow, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Novogrudok. I stood with some people there for a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they're alive anymore. [SIGHS] They're dead by now. Stood with them. I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some family and some not. And then very shortly after that, my brothers came.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not all live together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is together?

speaker: interviewer
text: The forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we lived together the forest. But you asked me where was I in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said your, your brothers came, where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, later on, they, they-- my brothers were not in Novogrudok. They were in other places, in other cities. I was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all-- 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: And second, my brothers were married. They had wives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the family you stayed with? Was it the family with children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange people, strange people that knew me, that knew us. That was no big deal, particularly right after the war. It was a privilege for one Jew to help the other at that time if he could with anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about living with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I don't want to repeat this anymore, but I was a very sad kid at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your brothers came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my brothers decided to quit Russia, to run away from Russia. They came. They took me. And we run away from Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ran away to Western Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, did you ever go back to your old house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were going frequently to our house out of the wood to see at night, you know, while during the course of the war. After the war, I didn't want to go to the house, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What am I going to go over there? Right after the war, no. I did not want to see the house. It was heartbreaking. Did you ever see any of your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: 40 years later, 35 years later, I went to see them, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hold that for a moment. And where did you and your brothers go when they decided to leave Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wind up in Romania, from Romania to Yugoslavia, from Yugoslavia to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first town in Italy, I think, was Trieste, if I remember correctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was with a group of other kids. And my brothers went to Israel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Palestine, in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was towards the end of '45. Now we're in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what type of a place were you with other kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I think those were Israeli people there that were sent from Palestine, from the Haganah, whatever. And, and then, you could get across a lot of Jewish kids or teenagers like me all over Europe, Western or Eastern, that have no home, that had nobody. Me, actually, I had brothers. But you had a lot like me that had no one. I was lucky that I had family in America. I had brothers. So I wasn't the worst off in the world yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of your family perished?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Nazis? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Oh, god, from the Nazis, four brothers, my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ones died from the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham, Yankel, Zisl, Isak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the place you stayed in Italy an orphanage or a youth camp of some kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was a small youth camp, which I was there very shortly. I left on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Trieste?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left from Italy to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just didn't want to stay there. I didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself, I went to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about the end of '45-- 15, 14,15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Where did I go in Paris?

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Where did--
